title: GitHub Actions
subtitle: CI/CD & Automation
description: Build CI/CD pipelines that test, lint, and deploy your code on every push.
icon: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" class="w-6 h-6" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke="currentColor"><path stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-width="2" d="M13 10V3L4 14h7v7l9-11h-7z" /></svg>

lessons.title: What is CI/CD & GitHub Actions?
lessons.goal: Understand Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, and the role GitHub Actions plays.
lessons.prerequisites: A basic understanding of Git repository commits and remote pushes.
lessons.whyMatters: Without CI/CD, developers must build, run tests, and deploy their applications manually. Humans make mistakes—forgetting to run tests, omitting environment variables, or pushing incorrect assets. CI/CD automates this entire flow, validating every single commit before it hits production.
lessons.guide:
The Concepts of CI/CD

Continuous Integration (CI): The practice of automatically building and testing your application every time a developer merges changes into the main branch. This catches integration bugs early.

Continuous Delivery/Deployment (CD): Pushing those successfully built and tested assets directly to production servers or hosting services without manual intervention.

GitHub Actions uses runner servers (hosted by GitHub or your own system) to execute commands defined in YAML files inside your repository's .github/workflows/ directory.

commands.explanation: Creates the standard directory structure where GitHub looks for automation scripts, and initializes a deploy workflow file.

errors.title: Workflow not appearing in GitHub 'Actions' tab
errors.solution: Verify that your YAML files are located precisely in the `.github/workflows/` directory. Spelled names matter: `.github` (singular) and `workflows` (plural). Ensure your files end with `.yml` or `.yaml`.

lessons.summary: You now understand how CI/CD automates builds, tests, and deployments, and where GitHub Actions workflow files live.

lessons.title: Workflows, Events & Runners
lessons.goal: Learn the YAML structure of a workflow and how to trigger jobs on specific repository events.
lessons.prerequisites: Understanding GitHub Actions directories and basic YAML syntax.
lessons.whyMatters: To automate effectively, you must control *when* your pipelines run. Triggering full tests on every commit is useful, but deploying should only happen when pushing to a protected branch like `main`. Defining events, jobs, and runners gives you this control.
lessons.guide:
Deconstructing a Workflow YAML

A workflow is composed of three core components:

  • Events (on): The triggers that start the workflow (e.g. push, pull_request, or scheduled cron jobs).
  • Jobs: A set of steps that execute on the same runner. Jobs run in parallel by default but can be configured to depend on each other.
  • Runners (runs-on): The environment/virtual machine that runs the job (e.g. ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, or macos-latest).

An interactive simulation of this execution is available below. Click 'Run Workflow' to see how the runner executes each job step.

commands.code:
name: Learn Workflows
on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: echo "Pipeline started!"
commands.explanation: A basic workflow template. It triggers when a push is made to the 'main' branch, runs on a fresh Ubuntu virtual machine, and prints a success statement.

errors.title: YAML Syntax Error: mapping values are not allowed here
errors.solution: YAML relies strictly on spacing and indentations, not tabs. Ensure you are using spaces, that colons have a space after them, and that list hyphens are correctly aligned.

lessons.summary: You learned how to write triggers and define virtual environments where your automated steps will execute.

lessons.title: Building a Build-and-Test Pipeline
lessons.goal: Build a workflow that checks out your code, installs dependencies, runs tests, and compiles the app.
lessons.whyMatters: Running compilation tests in isolation ensures that code compiles successfully in a clean environment, eliminating the 'works on my machine' issue. It guarantees that any broken code fails the build before affecting production.
lessons.guide:
Pre-made Actions & Tasks

Instead of writing commands for everything, you can reuse community actions using the uses key. Two of the most important actions are:

  • actions/checkout@v4: Clones your repository code into the runner's workspace directory.
  • actions/setup-node@v4: Installs Node.js and npm on the runner.

Once set up, you use the run command to install packages, run tests, and build assets.

commands.code:
name: Node Build & Test
on: [push]
jobs:
  test-job:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 18
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
      - run: npm run build
commands.explanation: Full workflow. It checks out the code, configures Node.js 18, runs clean install (npm ci) for packages, executes tests, and builds the production bundles.

errors.title: Process completed with exit code 1 / Test failure
errors.solution: GitHub Actions treats any step returning a non-zero exit code as a failure, halting subsequent steps. Read the console outputs of the failing step to locate syntax errors, missing dependencies, or failing unit test assertions.

lessons.summary: You built a complete CI verification pipeline that automatically validates your project codebase on every commit.
